news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advances in fundamental physics, innovative light-based AI power sources, novel concepts of time, and cutting-edge robotics revolutionizing manufacturing.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires en physique fondamentale, des sources d'énergie innovantes pour l'IA basées sur la lumière, de nouveaux concepts du temps et une robotique de pointe qui révolutionne le secteur manufacturier.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en física fundamental, innovadoras fuentes de energía para la inteligencia artificial basadas en la luz, nuevos conceptos sobre el tiempo y una robótica de vanguardia que está revolucionando la industria manufacturera.

articles.title: String theory suddenly emerged from simple physics rules
articles.summary: Physicists may have uncovered a surprising new clue that string theory—the idea that the universe is built from unimaginably tiny vibrating strings—could be more than just a mathematical fantasy. Instead of assuming strings existed from the start, researchers began with a few simple rules about how particles behave at extreme energies and discovered that the equations naturally produced the telltale fingerprints of string theory all on their own.
articles.content:
If you kept dividing an apple into smaller and smaller pieces, you would eventually reach molecules, then atoms, and later the tiny particles inside atoms such as protons, quarks, and gluons. But according to string theory, the journey does not stop there. At scales roughly a billion billion times smaller than a proton, physicists propose that everything may be made of incredibly tiny vibrating strings.

String theory first emerged in the 1960s as a possible way to solve one of physics' biggest problems: combining quantum mechanics, which governs the smallest particles, with general relativity, Einstein's theory describing gravity and the large-scale structure of the universe. Scientists have long struggled to unite the two because the equations often spiral into mathematical infinities when gravity is included at quantum scales.

String theory offers a potential way around that problem. In the theory, every particle, including the hypothetical graviton that would carry the force of gravity, comes from different vibrations of tiny strings. The mathematics also requires the strings to exist in at least 10 dimensions rather than the four dimensions humans experience.

One major obstacle remains. Testing string theory directly would require energies so extreme that researchers would need a particle collider as large as a galaxy.

Bootstrap Physics and String Theory

Since direct experiments are impossible with current technology, physicists are exploring other methods. One promising strategy is known as the "bootstrap" approach. Instead of assuming a detailed theory from the start, scientists begin with a few broad principles they believe nature must obey and then determine what laws naturally emerge.

In a new study titled "Strings from Almost Nothing," accepted for publication in Physical Review Letters, researchers from Caltech, New York University, and Institut de Fisica d'Altes Energies in Barcelona used this strategy to investigate particle behavior at extremely high energies. Starting from just a couple of assumptions about how particles scatter during collisions, they unexpectedly arrived at the core features of string theory.

"The strings just fell out," says Clifford Cheung, professor of theoretical physics and director of the Leinweber Forum for Theoretical Physics at Caltech. "We didn't start with any assumptions about strings at all, but then the solution contained the cornerstone signatures of strings."

Although the findings do not prove string theory experimentally, Cheung says the results are striking because many different mathematical outcomes could have been possible. Instead, the calculations pointed toward only one solution.

The Infinite Tower of Particles

One of the most important features to emerge from the calculations is known as the string spectrum. In the late 1960s, Italian theoretical physicist Gabriele Veneziano at CERN developed a mathematical function describing a mysterious "tower" of particles seen in collider experiments. The particles appeared in a sequence where mass and spin increased in orderly steps.

"At Veneziano's time, particle colliders were seeing this spray of junk come out of the collisions, particles of different masses. It was fascinating and nobody had any idea what was going on. Veneziano wrote down a function to describe all the masses, revealing an infinite tower of particles," Cheung says.

Researchers later realized this pattern resembles the harmonics of a vibrating string. When a violin string is plucked, it produces a main tone along with a series of overtones. String theory proposes that particles arise from similar vibrational patterns.

In 1974, Caltech physicist John Schwarz and French physicist Joël Scherk recognized that string theory could also include gravity. That discovery created one of the first meaningful links between string theory and general relativity.

"Like all particle physicists in that era, we had no prior interest in gravity. String theories are well-behaved at very high energies, unlike Einstein's general theory of relativity, which survives as a low-energy approximation. Therefore, even though much was not yet understood, we were very excited that some version of string theory could provide a unified quantum theory of everything," Schwarz says.

According to string theory, different vibrational modes generate different particles. A photon, for example, comes from an open string vibrating in its simplest mode, while the graviton is thought to arise from a closed vibrating string.

Why Quantum Gravity Breaks Down

The new study focused on scattering amplitudes, mathematical expressions describing the outcomes of particle collisions. When scientists use general relativity to calculate collisions at extremely high energies near the Planck scale, the math stops working properly and produces infinities.

"If you take general relativity and scatter at very high energies at the so-called Planck scale -- that is roughly 19 orders of magnitude greater than a proton's mass -- you get a result that makes no sense. Everything completely breaks down," Cheung says.

String theory avoids these infinities through a property called ultrasoftness. At extremely high energies, the strings effectively spread interactions out, preventing the violent behavior that normally causes the equations to fail.

"In a string theory framework, as you increase the energy transfer between particles, you will see a swift fall off in the probability that the particles will scatter. It's like the particles don't even want to scatter off one another, but rather pass freely," Cheung says. "The scattering amplitudes don't go to infinity. It's better behaved."

The researchers used this ultrasoft behavior as one of their starting assumptions. They also included another condition called "minimal zeros," which limits the number of points where scattering probabilities vanish.

"Remarkably, consistency requires scattering amplitudes not only to interact but also to not interact at special kinematic points called 'zeros.' The assumption of 'minimal zeros' demands the sparsest number of such vanishing points mathematically allowed by the equations," Cheung says.

Using only these assumptions, the team showed that the resulting mathematics naturally reproduced the defining characteristics of string theory, including its famous spectrum of particle masses and spins.

"The precise details of string theory emerged automatically, including the infinite tower of massive spinning particles that form the 'harmonics' of the string that the theory is famous for," says co-author Grant N. Remmen (PhD '17), the James Arthur Postdoctoral Fellow at New York University.

Reviving an Old Idea With Modern Tools

Cheung compares the bootstrap approach to solving a sudoku puzzle. A few simple rules are provided at the start, and those rules eventually guide you to one unique solution.

"The deep irony is that this bootstrap idea that we're pursuing now with modern tools and modern ideas is super retro. It's an old idea," Cheung explains. "The original discovery of the Veneziano spectrum, and John Schwarz's work, took a similar approach. They didn't start with string theory models but rather the solutions came out of basic principles."

The study also builds on earlier work by Caltech physicist Steven Frautschi and UC Berkeley physicist Geoffrey Chew, who pioneered the bootstrap approach in particle physics during the 1960s. Their work provided some of the earliest hints of the infinite particle spectrum later connected to string theory.

"The bootstrap idea had become obsolete but now people like Cliff are reviving and modernizing it," says Hirosi Ooguri, the Fred Kavli Professor of Theoretical Physics and Mathematics at Caltech and the Kent and Joyce Kresa Leadership Chair of the Division of Physics, Mathematics and Astronomy. "We now have a better understanding of the basic assumptions we can make, as well as stronger techniques for translating these assumptions into properties of scattering amplitudes and other observables."

The study "Strings from Almost Nothing" received funding from the US Department of Energy, the Walter Burke Institute for Theoretical Physics, the Leinweber Forum for Theoretical Physics, the James Arthur Postdoctoral Fellowship at New York University, and the Next Generation EU. Additional authors include Francesco Sciotti of Institut de Fisica d'Altes Energies in Barcelona and Michele Tarquini, a graduate student at Caltech.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant theoretical physics breakthrough where researchers derived string theory features from minimal assumptions about particle behavior, potentially advancing our understanding of the universe's fundamental nature. This discovery has broad scientific significance beyond a niche audience, offers substantial context and detail, and represents meaningful progress in unifying quantum mechanics and gravity.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find string theory emerges from simple physics rules
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – String theory suggests that the tiniest parts of the universe are actually tiny vibrating strings, much smaller than atoms or particles like quarks. It was created to help solve a big problem in physics: how to combine the rules of very small things (quantum mechanics) with gravity, which works on large things like planets and stars.

**What happened** – A team of physicists from Caltech, New York University, and a research institute in Barcelona used a method called the "bootstrap" approach. Instead of starting with string theory, they began with just a few simple ideas about how particles behave during collisions at very high energies. From these basic rules, they found that the math naturally showed the key features of string theory, including an infinite set of particles like the vibrations of a string.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows string theory might not just be a complicated idea made up by scientists, but something that naturally comes from basic physics. It helps connect two big areas of science that have been hard to join: quantum mechanics and gravity. The discovery also revives an old idea with new tools, giving physicists a clearer way to explore the universe’s smallest parts.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue to use the bootstrap method to learn more about particle behavior and test if these mathematical results can lead to real experiments in the future. They hope this work will guide new ways to understand the universe’s fundamental laws, even if building huge particle colliders is currently impossible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Physicists found that the main ideas of string theory can emerge naturally from just a few simple rules about particles, opening new paths to understand the universe’s smallest building blocks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des physiciens découvrent que la théorie des cordes émerge de règles simples
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La théorie des cordes propose que les plus petites parties de l’univers sont en fait de minuscules cordes vibrantes, bien plus petites que les atomes ou les particules comme les quarks. Elle a été créée pour résoudre un grand problème en physique : comment combiner les règles qui gouvernent les très petites choses (la mécanique quantique) avec la gravité, qui agit sur de grandes choses comme les planètes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de physiciens de Caltech, de l’Université de New York et d’un institut de recherche à Barcelone a utilisé une méthode appelée « bootstrap ». Au lieu de partir de la théorie des cordes, ils ont commencé avec seulement quelques idées simples sur le comportement des particules lors de collisions à très haute énergie. À partir de ces règles de base, ils ont découvert que les mathématiques montraient naturellement les caractéristiques principales de la théorie des cordes, y compris une série infinie de particules ressemblant aux vibrations d’une corde.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que la théorie des cordes n’est peut-être pas qu’une idée compliquée inventée par des scientifiques, mais quelque chose qui découle naturellement de la physique de base. Cela aide à relier deux grands domaines scientifiques difficiles à unir : la mécanique quantique et la gravité. Cette découverte redonne vie à une vieille idée avec des outils modernes, offrant aux physiciens une façon plus claire d’explorer les plus petites parties de l’univers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à utiliser la méthode bootstrap pour mieux comprendre le comportement des particules et voir si ces résultats mathématiques peuvent conduire à de véritables expériences. Ils espèrent que ce travail ouvrira de nouvelles voies pour comprendre les lois fondamentales de l’univers, même si construire de très grands collisionneurs de particules est actuellement impossible.

**En une phrase** – Les physiciens ont découvert que les idées principales de la théorie des cordes peuvent apparaître naturellement à partir de quelques règles simples sur les particules, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre les plus petites briques de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que la teoría de cuerdas surge de reglas simples
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La teoría de cuerdas dice que las partes más pequeñas del universo son diminutas cuerdas vibrantes, mucho más pequeñas que los átomos o partículas como los quarks. Fue creada para resolver un gran problema en la física: cómo juntar las reglas que gobiernan las cosas muy pequeñas (mecánica cuántica) con la gravedad, que funciona en cosas grandes como planetas.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de físicos de Caltech, la Universidad de Nueva York y un instituto de investigación en Barcelona usó un método llamado "bootstrap". En lugar de comenzar con la teoría de cuerdas, empezaron con solo unas pocas ideas simples sobre cómo se comportan las partículas durante colisiones a energías muy altas. De estas reglas básicas, encontraron que las matemáticas mostraban naturalmente las características clave de la teoría de cuerdas, incluyendo una serie infinita de partículas como las vibraciones de una cuerda.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la teoría de cuerdas podría no ser solo una idea complicada inventada por científicos, sino algo que surge de la física básica. Ayuda a conectar dos grandes áreas de la ciencia que han sido difíciles de unir: la mecánica cuántica y la gravedad. El descubrimiento también revive una idea antigua con herramientas modernas, dando a los físicos una forma más clara de explorar las partes más pequeñas del universo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán usando el método bootstrap para aprender más sobre el comportamiento de las partículas y ver si estos resultados matemáticos pueden llevar a experimentos reales en el futuro. Esperan que este trabajo guíe nuevas formas de entender las leyes fundamentales del universo, aunque construir grandes colisionadores de partículas sea imposible ahora.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los físicos encontraron que las ideas principales de la teoría de cuerdas pueden surgir naturalmente de solo unas pocas reglas simples sobre las partículas, abriendo nuevos caminos para entender las partes más pequeñas del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an intricate cosmic violin string gently vibrating amidst a softly glowing infinite tower of translucent, harmonically arranged particles rising upward, set against a serene abstract backdrop evoking the deep fabric of the universe in natural, muted tones.

articles.title: Forget electrons, this breakthrough uses light-matter particles to power AI
articles.summary: Researchers at Penn have created a hybrid light-matter particle that could dramatically speed up AI computing while using far less energy. The breakthrough may help replace some electronic computing processes with ultra-efficient light-based technology.
articles.content:
Eighty years after the creation of ENIAC, the world's first general-purpose electronic computer, researchers at the University of Pennsylvania are exploring a new way to power the future of computing. Instead of relying entirely on electrons, which have formed the backbone of computers since the 1940s, scientists are now turning to light.

ENIAC, developed by Penn researchers J. Presper Eckert and John Mauchly, helped launch the modern computing era by using streams of electrons to solve complex mathematical problems. That same electronic approach still powers today's computers, smartphones, and AI systems. But as artificial intelligence grows more demanding, the limits of electron-based hardware are becoming harder to ignore.

Why Electrons Are Reaching Their Limits

Electrons carry an electrical charge, which creates several challenges inside modern computer chips. As they move through materials, they generate heat and face resistance that wastes energy. Those problems become even more difficult as chips grow more complex and process enormous amounts of data for AI applications.

Researchers led by Penn physicist Bo Zhen in the School of Arts & Sciences believe photons, the particles that make up light, could help solve some of these issues.

"Because they are charge-neutral and have zero rest mass, photons can carry information quickly over long distances with minimal loss, dominating communications technology," explains Li He, co-first author of a paper published in Physical Review Letters and a former postdoctoral researcher in the Zhen Lab. "But that neutrality means they barely interact with their environment, making them bad at the sort of signal-switching logic that computers depend on."

In other words, light is excellent for carrying information quickly and efficiently, but it struggles with the switching operations needed for computing.

Combining Light and Matter for AI Computing

To overcome that problem, Zhen's team developed a special quasiparticle called an exciton-polariton. The particle forms when photons are strongly linked with electrons inside an atomically thin semiconductor material. This combination allows light to interact much more effectively, making it capable of performing the signal switching required for computing tasks.

The breakthrough could be especially important for artificial intelligence systems, which consume enormous amounts of power.

Many experimental photonic AI chips already use light to handle certain calculations at high speed. However, when these systems need to perform nonlinear activation steps, such as decision-making operations, they usually must convert light signals back into electronic ones. That conversion slows the process and increases energy use, reducing the benefits of photonic computing.

Using exciton-polaritons, the Penn researchers demonstrated all-light switching while using only about 4 quadrillionths of a joule of energy. That amount is extraordinarily small, far below the energy needed to briefly power a tiny LED light.

Toward Faster and More Efficient AI Chips

If the technology can be successfully scaled, it could lead to photonic chips capable of processing information directly from cameras without repeated conversions between light and electricity. The approach could also lower the massive energy demands of large AI systems and potentially support basic quantum computing functions on future chips.

Bo Zhen is the Jin K. Lee Presidential Associate Professor in the Department of Physics and Astronomy in the School of Arts & Sciences at the University of Pennsylvania.

Li He was a postdoctoral researcher in the Zhen Lab in Penn Arts & Sciences. He is currently an assistant professor at Montana State University.

Additional authors on the study include Zhi Wang and Bumho Kim from the University of Pennsylvania's School of Arts & Sciences.

The research was supported by the US Office of Naval Research (N00014-20-1-2325 and N00014-21-1-2703) and the Sloan Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in computing technology using hybrid light-matter particles to dramatically speed up AI computing while reducing energy consumption. This innovation addresses the broad challenge of energy inefficiency in AI hardware, with potential large-scale benefits for society by enabling faster, more efficient AI systems and lowering environmental impact. The article provides detailed context on the discovery, its mechanism, and its implications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New light-based particles could make AI faster and use less energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Computers today rely on electrons to process information, but as artificial intelligence (AI) systems grow more complex, this method uses a lot of energy and creates heat. Researchers at the University of Pennsylvania are looking for better ways to power future computers.

**What happened** – A team led by physicist Bo Zhen developed a new particle called an exciton-polariton, which combines light particles (photons) with electrons inside a very thin material. This hybrid particle can switch signals like electrons do but uses much less energy. The team showed that these particles can perform computing tasks using only a tiny fraction of the energy normally needed.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it could lead to AI chips that work much faster and use far less power than current ones. Unlike other light-based computing attempts, this method avoids converting light signals back to electricity, which wastes time and energy. It could help reduce the huge energy demands of AI and even support future quantum computing.

**What's next step** – Researchers will try to build larger, working AI chips using this light-matter technology. If successful, these chips might process information directly from cameras and other sensors without extra energy-heavy conversions. This could change how AI devices are designed and make them more efficient.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created a new particle that uses light and matter to make AI computing faster and much more energy-efficient than current technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles particules à base de lumière pourraient accélérer l'IA tout en consommant moins d'énergie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs actuels utilisent des électrons pour traiter les informations, mais à mesure que l'intelligence artificielle (IA) devient plus complexe, cette méthode consomme beaucoup d'énergie et génère de la chaleur. Des chercheurs de l'Université de Pennsylvanie cherchent des solutions meilleures pour alimenter les ordinateurs du futur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par le physicien Bo Zhen a développé une nouvelle particule appelée exciton-polariton, qui combine des particules de lumière (photons) avec des électrons dans un matériau très fin. Cette particule hybride peut commuter des signaux comme les électrons, mais utilise beaucoup moins d'énergie. L'équipe a montré que ces particules peuvent effectuer des tâches informatiques en utilisant une infime fraction de l'énergie normalement nécessaire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle pourrait permettre de créer des puces d'IA beaucoup plus rapides et bien moins énergivores que celles d'aujourd'hui. Contrairement à d'autres tentatives de calcul utilisant la lumière, cette méthode évite de convertir les signaux lumineux en électricité, ce qui fait perdre du temps et de l'énergie. Cela pourrait réduire la forte demande énergétique de l'IA et même soutenir l'informatique quantique du futur.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont essayer de construire des puces d'IA plus grandes utilisant cette technologie lumière-matière. Si cela réussit, ces puces pourraient traiter directement les informations venant des caméras et autres capteurs sans conversions énergivores. Cela pourrait changer la conception des appareils d'IA et les rendre plus efficaces.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé une nouvelle particule qui utilise la lumière et la matière pour rendre le calcul de l'IA plus rapide et beaucoup plus économe en énergie que la technologie actuelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas partículas basadas en luz podrían hacer la IA más rápida y con menos energía
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras hoy en día usan electrones para procesar información, pero a medida que los sistemas de inteligencia artificial (IA) se vuelven más complejos, este método consume mucha energía y genera calor. Investigadores de la Universidad de Pensilvania buscan mejores formas de alimentar las computadoras del futuro.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por el físico Bo Zhen desarrolló una nueva partícula llamada excitón-polaritón, que combina partículas de luz (fotones) con electrones dentro de un material muy delgado. Esta partícula híbrida puede cambiar señales como los electrones, pero usa mucha menos energía. El equipo demostró que estas partículas pueden realizar tareas de computación usando solo una pequeña fracción de la energía normalmente necesaria.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque podría llevar a chips de IA que funcionen mucho más rápido y consuman mucha menos energía que los actuales. A diferencia de otros intentos de computación con luz, este método evita convertir señales de luz de nuevo en electricidad, lo que consume tiempo y energía. Esto podría ayudar a reducir las enormes demandas energéticas de la IA e incluso apoyar la computación cuántica futura.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores intentarán construir chips de IA más grandes usando esta tecnología de luz y materia. Si tienen éxito, estos chips podrían procesar información directamente desde cámaras y otros sensores sin conversiones que consumen mucha energía. Esto podría cambiar cómo se diseñan los dispositivos de IA y hacerlos más eficientes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos crearon una nueva partícula que usa luz y materia para hacer que la computación de IA sea más rápida y mucho más eficiente en energía que la tecnología actual.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, atom-thin semiconductor layer gently woven with radiant light beams transforming into intertwined streams of electrons and photons, symbolizing the fusion of light-matter particles powering a futuristic AI chip, rendered in soft natural hues of amber, soft blue, and gentle white.

articles.title: Schrödinger’s clock: Time could tick faster and slower at the same time
articles.summary: Time might be even stranger than Einstein imagined. Physicists are now exploring the possibility that a single clock could exist in a quantum superposition, ticking both faster and slower at the same time — almost like Schrödinger’s cat being both alive and dead simultaneously. Using incredibly precise atomic clocks and cutting-edge quantum technologies, researchers believe they may soon be able to test this bizarre prediction in the lab for the first time.
articles.content:
Few concepts in physics are as familiar, or as puzzling, as time itself. Einstein's theory of relativity showed that time is not fixed or universal. Instead, it changes depending on speed and gravity. When scientists combine relativity with quantum mechanics, however, the picture becomes even stranger. Quantum theory suggests that time itself could exist in a superposition, meaning it may flow both faster and slower simultaneously.

A newly published study suggests researchers may soon be able to test this bizarre idea in the lab. The paper, titled "Quantum signatures of proper time in optical ion clocks," appeared April 20, 2026 in Physical Review Letters. The research was led by Assistant Professor Igor Pikovski of Stevens Institute of Technology, working with experimental teams led by Christian Sanner at Colorado State University and Dietrich Leibfried at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST).

Atomic Clocks and Quantum Time

The researchers explored how advanced atomic clocks could reveal hidden quantum effects connected to the flow of time. According to their findings, the same technologies being developed for next generation clocks and quantum computers may also allow scientists to investigate whether time itself behaves according to quantum rules.

In quantum mechanics, objects can exist in multiple states at once. This concept is famously illustrated by Schrödinger's cat, a thought experiment in which a cat is considered both alive and dead simultaneously until it is observed. The researchers suggest something similarly strange could happen with time. A clock whose motion follows quantum rules could experience multiple flows of time at the same moment, almost like a cat that is both young and old simultaneously.

"Time plays very different roles in quantum theory and in relativity," says Pikovski. "What we show is that bringing these two concepts together can reveal hidden quantum signatures of time-flow that can no longer be described by classical physics."

The Quantum Twin Paradox

Relativity already predicts that time passes differently depending on motion and location. Every clock measures its own unique flow of time. For instance, a clock traveling at 10 m/s for 57 million years would fall behind a stationary clock by about one second. Scientists have confirmed effects like this using highly precise devices such as aluminum-ion clocks at NIST.

This phenomenon is often explained using the "twin paradox," where one twin travels at high speed and returns younger than the twin who stayed behind. The new study pushes this idea further into the quantum realm.

Researchers asked whether a single clock could experience two different rates of time simultaneously while existing in a quantum superposition. According to quantum theory, that should be possible. Pikovski and his collaborators first proposed the idea more than a decade ago, but the effect was too subtle to observe experimentally at the time. Advances in atomic clock technology may now change that.

Ultracold Ion Clocks and Quantum Fluctuations

The team focused on ion clocks like those being developed at NIST and Colorado State University. These devices trap single ions such as aluminum or ytterbium, cool them to temperatures near absolute zero, and control their quantum states using lasers.

Their analysis showed that combining highly accurate clocks with techniques from trapped-ion quantum computing could make it possible to observe previously hidden quantum properties of time.

"Atomic clocks are now so sensitive, they can detect tiny differences in time caused by just the thermal vibrations at minuscule temperatures," says Gabriel Sorci, a PhD candidate at Stevens Institute of Technology and co-author of the paper. "But even at the absolute zero temperature, the ground state, the ticking rate will still be affected by just the quantum fluctuations alone."

The researchers then explored an even more unusual possibility. Instead of simply cooling the atoms, they proposed manipulating the vacuum itself by creating "squeezed states," quantum states in which position and velocity behave in unusual ways.

Clocks That Tick Faster and Slower at Once

Under these conditions, the team found that entirely new quantum effects involving time could emerge. A single clock could effectively tick both faster and slower at the same time while also becoming entangled with its own quantum motion.

The researchers now hope to demonstrate these effects experimentally.

"We have the technology to generate the required squeezing and a path to reach the clock precision needed in ion clocks to observe such effects for the first time," says Sanner of Colorado State.

For Pikovski, the broader implications are just as exciting. His previous work includes showing that quantum technology could potentially detect single gravitons, the hypothetical particles thought to carry gravity.

"Physics is still full of mysteries at the most fundamental level. Quantum technologies are now giving us new tools to shed light on them."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in quantum physics and atomic clock technology, with researchers poised to experimentally test a groundbreaking prediction about the nature of time. This breakthrough has broad implications for fundamental physics and quantum technology, offering a meaningful and inspiring real-world impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists prepare to test if a clock can tick faster and slower at once
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists know that time is not fixed. Einstein showed that time can move slower or faster depending on speed and gravity. Now, physicists are exploring an even stranger idea: that a single clock might tick both faster and slower at the same time, thanks to quantum physics.

**What happened** – A new study led by Professor Igor Pikovski and teams at Colorado State University and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) says this strange idea could soon be tested in a lab. They plan to use advanced atomic clocks that trap and cool single ions (charged atoms) to almost absolute zero. These clocks are so precise they can detect tiny differences in time caused by quantum effects. The researchers suggest that under special quantum conditions, a clock could exist in a state where it ticks at two different speeds simultaneously.

**Impact** – This is important because it combines two big ideas in physics: relativity (how time changes with speed and gravity) and quantum mechanics (how things can be in multiple states at once). The idea is similar to Schrödinger’s cat, a famous thought experiment where a cat can be both alive and dead at the same time. Testing this with clocks could show new hidden properties of time that classical physics can’t explain. It may also improve our understanding of the universe and help develop quantum technologies.

**What's next step** – The research teams aim to experimentally observe these quantum effects using ion clocks and special techniques to control their quantum states. They believe current technology can create the right conditions to see a clock ticking both faster and slower at once. Successfully doing this would open new doors for studying time and quantum physics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers are close to testing whether a single atomic clock can tick at two different speeds at the same time, revealing new quantum properties of time itself.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques prêts à tester si une horloge peut avancer et retarder en même temps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques savent que le temps n’est pas fixe. Einstein a montré que le temps peut ralentir ou accélérer selon la vitesse et la gravité. Aujourd’hui, des physiciens explorent une idée encore plus étrange : qu’une seule horloge pourrait avancer et retarder en même temps, grâce à la physique quantique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude dirigée par le professeur Igor Pikovski et des équipes de l’Université d’État du Colorado et de l’Institut national des normes et de la technologie (NIST) affirme que cette idée étrange pourrait bientôt être testée en laboratoire. Ils prévoient d’utiliser des horloges atomiques avancées qui piègent et refroidissent des ions simples (atomes chargés) presque jusqu’au zéro absolu. Ces horloges sont si précises qu’elles peuvent détecter de minuscules différences de temps causées par des effets quantiques. Les chercheurs suggèrent que dans des conditions quantiques spéciales, une horloge pourrait exister dans un état où elle avance à deux vitesses différentes en même temps.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela combine deux grandes idées en physique : la relativité (comment le temps change selon la vitesse et la gravité) et la mécanique quantique (comment les objets peuvent être dans plusieurs états en même temps). L’idée est similaire au chat de Schrödinger, une expérience de pensée célèbre où un chat peut être à la fois vivant et mort. Tester cela avec des horloges pourrait révéler de nouvelles propriétés cachées du temps que la physique classique ne peut pas expliquer. Cela pourrait aussi améliorer notre compréhension de l’univers et aider à développer des technologies quantiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes de recherche veulent observer expérimentalement ces effets quantiques en utilisant des horloges à ions et des techniques spéciales pour contrôler leurs états quantiques. Ils pensent que la technologie actuelle peut créer les bonnes conditions pour voir une horloge avancer et retarder en même temps. Réussir cela ouvrirait de nouvelles voies pour étudier le temps et la physique quantique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chercheurs sont proches de tester si une horloge atomique unique peut avancer à deux vitesses différentes en même temps, révélant de nouvelles propriétés quantiques du temps lui-même.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos se preparan para probar si un reloj puede ir más rápido y más lento a la vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos saben que el tiempo no es fijo. Einstein mostró que el tiempo puede ir más lento o más rápido según la velocidad y la gravedad. Ahora, los físicos exploran una idea aún más extraña: que un solo reloj podría ir más rápido y más lento al mismo tiempo, gracias a la física cuántica.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio dirigido por el profesor Igor Pikovski y equipos de la Universidad Estatal de Colorado y el Instituto Nacional de Estándares y Tecnología (NIST) dice que esta extraña idea pronto podría probarse en laboratorio. Planean usar relojes atómicos avanzados que atrapan y enfrían iones individuales (átomos cargados) casi hasta el cero absoluto. Estos relojes son tan precisos que pueden detectar pequeñas diferencias en el tiempo causadas por efectos cuánticos. Los investigadores sugieren que bajo condiciones cuánticas especiales, un reloj podría existir en un estado donde marca dos velocidades diferentes al mismo tiempo.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque combina dos grandes ideas en física: la relatividad (cómo cambia el tiempo según la velocidad y la gravedad) y la mecánica cuántica (cómo los objetos pueden estar en varios estados a la vez). La idea es similar al gato de Schrödinger, un experimento mental famoso donde un gato está vivo y muerto a la vez. Probar esto con relojes podría mostrar nuevas propiedades ocultas del tiempo que la física clásica no puede explicar. También podría mejorar nuestra comprensión del universo y ayudar a desarrollar tecnologías cuánticas.

**Próximo paso** – Los equipos de investigación quieren observar estos efectos cuánticos experimentalmente usando relojes de iones y técnicas especiales para controlar sus estados cuánticos. Creen que la tecnología actual puede crear las condiciones necesarias para ver un reloj que marca más rápido y más lento al mismo tiempo. Lograr esto abriría nuevas puertas para estudiar el tiempo y la física cuántica.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores están cerca de probar si un solo reloj atómico puede marcar a dos velocidades diferentes al mismo tiempo, revelando nuevas propiedades cuánticas del tiempo mismo.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, softly glowing atomic clock with delicate ion orbits suspended inside a translucent sphere, its hands simultaneously blurred and sharp to symbolize ticking both faster and slower, set against a serene abstract background of intertwined wave-like quantum patterns and subtle light flares suggesting superposition and entanglement, rendered in warm, natural tones with fine, detailed brushstrokes.

articles.title: Robo-top: The machines that could make your next t-shirt
articles.summary: Most clothes are made in Asia, but new machines could bring some of that work back to the West.
articles.content: His company takes another approach. Forget sewing – glue the pieces of fabric together instead. "Once the adhesive is laid down, you simply line something over it and stamp." CreateMe has designed robots that do this and the firm is already making women's underwear this way. It will begin producing t-shirts, too, in the coming months. Mass production could follow next year.
articles.reasoning: The article describes an innovative manufacturing technology using robots and adhesives to produce clothing, which could bring textile production back to Western countries. This has potential broad economic and social benefits by reshoring jobs and advancing manufacturing methods.
articles.personality_title: New robots could bring T-shirt making back to the West
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most clothes today are made in factories in Asia. This has been the case for many years because of lower costs there.

**What happened** – A company called CreateMe has invented robots that make clothes using glue instead of sewing. These robots stick pieces of fabric together by putting down adhesive and pressing the pieces together. The company is already using this method to make women’s underwear and plans to start making T-shirts soon.

**Impact** – This new way of making clothes could allow factories in Western countries to start producing clothing again. It could create jobs closer to home and use new technology to make the process faster and maybe cheaper. This is different from traditional sewing and could change how clothes are made.

**What's next step** – CreateMe hopes to begin mass production of T-shirts with these robots next year. If successful, more clothing factories might adopt this technology, bringing more work back to the West.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Robots that glue fabric instead of sewing could help bring clothing production back to Western countries and create new jobs.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux robots pourraient relancer la fabrication de t-shirts en Occident
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aujourd’hui, la plupart des vêtements sont fabriqués dans des usines en Asie. Cela dure depuis de nombreuses années en raison des coûts plus bas.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise nommée CreateMe a inventé des robots qui fabriquent des vêtements en collant les morceaux de tissu plutôt qu’en cousant. Ces robots déposent de la colle et pressent les pièces ensemble. La société utilise déjà cette méthode pour fabriquer des sous-vêtements féminins et prévoit de commencer à produire des t-shirts bientôt.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle façon de fabriquer des vêtements pourrait permettre aux usines occidentales de recommencer à produire des habits. Cela pourrait créer des emplois plus près de chez soi et utiliser une technologie nouvelle pour rendre le processus plus rapide et peut-être moins cher. C’est différent de la couture traditionnelle et cela pourrait changer la fabrication des vêtements.

**Prochaine étape** – CreateMe espère lancer la production en masse de t-shirts avec ces robots l’année prochaine. Si cela marche, davantage d’usines pourraient adopter cette technologie et ramener du travail en Occident.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des robots qui collent le tissu au lieu de le coudre pourraient aider à relocaliser la production de vêtements en Occident et créer de nouveaux emplois.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos robots podrían traer la fabricación de camisetas de vuelta a Occidente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hoy en día, la mayoría de la ropa se fabrica en fábricas en Asia. Esto ha sido así durante muchos años debido a los costos más bajos.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada CreateMe ha inventado robots que hacen ropa usando pegamento en lugar de coser. Estos robots aplican adhesivo y presionan las piezas de tela juntas. La empresa ya usa este método para fabricar ropa interior femenina y planea empezar a fabricar camisetas pronto.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva forma de hacer ropa podría permitir que las fábricas en países occidentales vuelvan a producir prendas. Podría crear empleos más cerca de casa y usar tecnología nueva para hacer el proceso más rápido y quizás más barato. Esto es diferente a la costura tradicional y podría cambiar la forma en que se hacen las prendas.

**Próximo paso** – CreateMe espera comenzar la producción en masa de camisetas con estos robots el próximo año. Si tiene éxito, más fábricas podrían adoptar esta tecnología y traer más trabajo a Occidente.

**Resumen en una frase** – Robots que pegan la tela en lugar de coserla podrían ayudar a traer la producción de ropa de vuelta a los países occidentales y crear nuevos empleos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek robotic arm gently pressing two colorful fabric pieces together with a glowing adhesive layer between them, set against a soft, neutral background, symbolizing innovative, seamless garment creation without sewing.
